news_summary: Today's technology news highlights groundbreaking advances in quantum physics and materials science, including novel quantum phenomena and innovative aluminum alloys with the potential to reduce reliance on rare metals and transform our understanding of reality.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées révolutionnaires en physique quantique et en science des matériaux, notamment de nouveaux phénomènes quantiques et des alliages d'aluminium innovants susceptibles de réduire notre dépendance aux métaux rares et de transformer notre compréhension de la réalité.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan avances revolucionarios en física cuántica y ciencia de los materiales, entre los que se incluyen fenómenos cuánticos novedosos y aleaciones de aluminio innovadoras con el potencial de reducir la dependencia de los metales raros y transformar nuestra comprensión de la realidad.

articles.title: Oxford physicists achieve first-ever “quadsqueezing” breakthrough in quantum physics
articles.summary: Scientists have created a powerful new way to control quantum systems, achieving the first-ever demonstration of quadsqueezing—an elusive fourth-order quantum effect. By combining simple forces in a clever way, they made previously hidden quantum behaviors visible and usable, opening new frontiers for quantum technology.
articles.content:
Researchers at the University of Oxford have demonstrated a new kind of quantum interaction using a single trapped ion. By carefully generating and controlling increasingly complex forms of "squeezing" -- including a fourth-order effect called quadsqueezing -- they have made quantum behaviors accessible that had previously been out of reach. The work also introduces a new way to engineer these interactions, with potential uses in quantum simulation, sensing, and computing. The findings were published today (May 1) in Nature Physics.

Many physical systems behave like tiny oscillating objects, similar to springs or pendulums. In quantum physics, these are known as quantum harmonic oscillators. This description applies to a wide range of systems, including light waves, molecular vibrations, and even the motion of a single trapped atom.

Controlling these oscillations is essential for modern quantum technologies. Applications range from extremely precise measurement tools to the development of next-generation quantum computers.

Squeezing and the Limits of Quantum Precision

One of the most common techniques for controlling quantum oscillators is called squeezing. Quantum mechanics places strict limits on how precisely certain pairs of properties, such as position and momentum, can be measured at the same time. Squeezing redistributes this uncertainty by making one property more precise while increasing uncertainty in the other.

This concept is not just theoretical. Squeezed light is already used in gravitational-wave detectors such as LIGO to enhance sensitivity.

Going Beyond Standard Squeezing

Standard squeezing is only one part of a broader set of possible interactions. Physicists have long aimed to create more complex versions, known as trisqueezing and quadsqueezing. These higher-order effects are much harder to achieve because they are naturally very weak and quickly become overwhelmed by noise.

As a result, observing these advanced quantum interactions has remained a major challenge.

A New Method Using Non-Commuting Forces

The Oxford team developed a solution by combining two precisely controlled forces acting on a single trapped ion. This approach builds on a theory proposed in 2021 by Dr. Raghavendra Srinivas and Robert Tyler Sutherland.

Each force on its own produces a simple, predictable effect. When applied together, however, they generate a stronger and more complex interaction. This happens because of non-commutativity, a quantum effect in which the order and combination of actions change the outcome, allowing the forces to amplify each other.

Lead author, Dr. Oana Băzăvan, Department of Physics, University of Oxford, said: "In the lab, non-commuting interactions are often seen as a nuisance because they introduce unwanted dynamics. Here, we took the opposite approach and used that feature to generate stronger quantum interactions."

First-Ever Demonstration of Quadsqueezing

Using the same experimental setup, the researchers were able to switch between different levels of squeezing. They successfully produced standard squeezing, trisqueezing, and, for the first time on any platform, quadsqueezing, a fourth-order interaction.

By adjusting the frequencies, phases, and strengths of the applied forces, they could control which interaction appeared while minimizing unwanted effects.

Dr. Oana Băzăvan said: "The result is more than the creation of a new quantum state. It is a demonstration of a new method for engineering interactions that were previously out of reach. The fourth-order quadsqueezing interaction was generated more than 100 times faster than expected using conventional approaches. This makes effects that were previously out of reach accessible in practice."

Confirming the Quantum Effects

To verify their results, the team reconstructed the quantum motion of the trapped ion. The measurements revealed distinct patterns corresponding to second-, third-, and fourth-order squeezing. These patterns provided clear evidence that each type of interaction had been successfully created.

Future Applications in Quantum Technology

The researchers are now extending this method to more complex systems with multiple modes of motion. Because the technique relies on tools already available in many quantum platforms, it could become a widely useful way to explore advanced quantum behavior.

The approach has already been combined with mid-circuit measurements of the ion's spin to generate flexible combinations of squeezed states and to simulate a lattice gauge theory.

Study co-author Dr. Raghavendra Srinivas (Department of Physics, University of Oxford), who supervised the work, said: "Fundamentally, we have demonstrated a new type of interaction that lets us explore quantum physics in uncharted territory, and we are genuinely excited for the discoveries to come."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in quantum physics with the first-ever demonstration of quadsqueezing, a complex quantum interaction. This achievement opens new possibilities for quantum technologies such as sensing, simulation, and computing, which have broad potential impact on science and technology. The article provides detailed context about the discovery, its novelty, and future applications, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Oxford scientists achieve first quadsqueezing in quantum physics
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum physics studies tiny particles and their strange behaviors. One way scientists control these particles is by squeezing their properties to measure them better. Until now, only simpler forms of squeezing were possible.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Oxford used a single trapped ion and two special forces to create a new, stronger kind of squeezing called quadsqueezing, a fourth-order effect never seen before. They carefully controlled the forces so they worked together to make this new quantum interaction visible and fast.

**Impact** – This is the first time quadsqueezing has been created in a lab. It shows a new way to control quantum systems that could improve quantum computers, sensors, and simulations. The method is faster and more flexible than older techniques, making complex quantum behaviors easier to study and use.

**What's next step** – The Oxford team plans to apply this method to more complex systems with many moving parts. Since the tools they used are common in quantum labs, other scientists could soon explore new quantum effects and develop advanced technologies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Oxford physicists have made a breakthrough by creating the first quadsqueezing effect, opening new paths to control and use complex quantum behaviors.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques d’Oxford réalisent la première quadsqueeze en physique quantique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La physique quantique étudie les particules très petites et leurs comportements étranges. Une façon pour les scientifiques de contrôler ces particules est de comprimer leurs propriétés pour mieux les mesurer. Jusqu’à présent, seules des formes plus simples de compression étaient possibles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université d’Oxford ont utilisé un ion piégé et deux forces spéciales pour créer une nouvelle forme plus puissante de compression appelée quadsqueeze, un effet de quatrième ordre jamais vu auparavant. Ils ont contrôlé précisément ces forces pour qu’elles agissent ensemble et rendent cette nouvelle interaction quantique visible et rapide.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que la quadsqueeze est créée en laboratoire. Cela montre une nouvelle façon de contrôler les systèmes quantiques qui pourrait améliorer les ordinateurs quantiques, les capteurs et les simulations. Cette méthode est plus rapide et flexible que les anciennes, facilitant l’étude et l’utilisation des comportements quantiques complexes.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe d’Oxford prévoit d’appliquer cette méthode à des systèmes plus complexes avec plusieurs parties en mouvement. Comme les outils utilisés sont courants dans les laboratoires quantiques, d’autres scientifiques pourraient bientôt explorer de nouveaux effets quantiques et développer des technologies avancées.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des physiciens d’Oxford ont réalisé une avancée en créant la première quadsqueeze, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour contrôler et utiliser des comportements quantiques complexes.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos de Oxford logran la primera quadsqueezing en física cuántica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La física cuántica estudia partículas muy pequeñas y sus extraños comportamientos. Una forma en que los científicos controlan estas partículas es comprimiendo sus propiedades para medirlas mejor. Hasta ahora, solo se podían hacer formas más simples de compresión.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Oxford usaron un ion atrapado y dos fuerzas especiales para crear un nuevo tipo más fuerte de compresión llamado quadsqueezing, un efecto de cuarto orden nunca antes visto. Controlaron cuidadosamente las fuerzas para que trabajaran juntas y hicieran visible y rápido este nuevo tipo de interacción cuántica.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que se crea quadsqueezing en un laboratorio. Esto muestra una nueva manera de controlar sistemas cuánticos que podría mejorar computadoras cuánticas, sensores y simulaciones. El método es más rápido y flexible que los antiguos, facilitando el estudio y uso de comportamientos cuánticos complejos.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo de Oxford planea aplicar este método a sistemas más complejos con muchas partes en movimiento. Como las herramientas usadas son comunes en laboratorios cuánticos, otros científicos podrían pronto explorar nuevos efectos cuánticos y desarrollar tecnologías avanzadas.

**Frase clave** – Los físicos de Oxford han logrado un avance creando el primer efecto de quadsqueezing, abriendo nuevos caminos para controlar y usar comportamientos cuánticos complejos.
articles.image_prompt: A delicate, glowing single ion suspended at the center of a softly illuminated abstract quantum landscape, surrounded by layered, translucent oscillating waves and intricate interwoven loops that represent second-, third-, and fourth-order squeezing, rendered in warm, muted earth tones with fine, flowing lines conveying harmony and breakthrough in quantum control.

articles.title: This new aluminum could replace rare metals and cut costs dramatically
articles.summary: A team at King’s College London has created a powerful new aluminum compound capable of doing the work of expensive rare metals. Its unique triangular structure gives it remarkable stability and reactivity, allowing it to drive chemical reactions in ways never seen before. The discovery could lead to greener and far more affordable industrial processes. It may even enable the creation of entirely new materials.
articles.content:
A team of scientists at King's College London has identified a new form of aluminum, one of the most abundant metals on Earth, that could offer a far less expensive and more sustainable alternative to widely used rare earth metals.

Led by Dr. Clare Bakewell, a Senior Lecturer in the Department of Chemistry, the researchers created highly reactive aluminum molecules capable of breaking some of the strongest chemical bonds. Their findings, published in Nature Communications, also reveal entirely new molecular structures, opening the door to previously unknown types of chemical behavior.

Discovery of a Unique Aluminum Structure

The researchers reported the first known example of a cyclotrialumane, a compound made of three aluminum atoms arranged in a trimeric -- triangular -- structure. This unusual configuration shows remarkable reactivity. Importantly, the structure remains intact even when dissolved in different solutions, giving it the stability needed for a variety of chemical reactions.

These reactions include splitting dihydrogen and enabling the step-by-step insertion and chain growth of ethene, a simple 2-carbon hydrocarbon. Such capabilities highlight the compound's potential for building more complex molecules.

Replacing Expensive and Scarce Metals

Metals play a central role in producing both everyday and specialized chemicals used across industry. Many of these processes rely on precious metals like platinum, which are costly and can have significant environmental impacts due to extraction.

Scientists have been searching for alternatives that are easier to obtain and more sustainable. Dr. Clare Bakewell explained: "Transition metals are the workhorses of chemical synthesis and catalysis -- but many of the most useful are becoming increasingly difficult to access and extract -- often being located in regions of political instability, increasing the demand and price.

"Chemists have been looking towards more common elements from the periodic table, and we chose aluminum, as it's super abundant, making it ~20,000 times less expensive than precious metals such as platinum and palladium."

Expanding the Possibilities of Aluminum Chemistry

In addition to designing aluminum compounds for use in chemical synthesis, the team is uncovering entirely new reactions.

Dr. Bakewell said, "What's special about this work, is that we're pushing the boundaries of chemical knowledge. Most excitingly, we can use this aluminum trimer to build completely new compounds with levels of reactivity that have never been observed before -- these include the 5- and 7-membered aluminum and carbon rings formed through reaction with ethene. These capabilities go beyond the transition metals we were originally trying to mimic, to the forefront of chemical research."

This emerging chemistry could allow scientists to develop new reaction types or assemble larger molecular structures with distinctive properties, potentially leading to new materials and products.

Toward Cleaner and Cheaper Chemical Production

Bakewell emphasized that the research is still in its early stages but shows significant promise.

She said, "we're very much in the exploratory phase and we're just at the start of beginning to unlock the capability of these earth-abundant materials.

"But from what we've seen already, this chemistry could support a transition to cleaner, greener and cheaper chemical production, whilst making new discoveries along the way."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in chemistry with the discovery of a new aluminum compound that could replace rare and expensive metals in industrial processes. This has broad implications for sustainability, cost reduction, and the development of new materials, benefiting society at large. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights both the innovation and its potential positive impact on cleaner, greener chemical production.
articles.personality_title: New aluminum compound could replace rare metals and lower costs
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists often use rare and expensive metals like platinum in making chemicals and materials. These metals are costly and can be hard to find because they come from unstable regions. Researchers have been looking for cheaper and more common alternatives.

**What happened** – A team at King’s College London, led by Dr. Clare Bakewell, discovered a new form of aluminum with a unique triangular shape made of three aluminum atoms. This new compound is very reactive and stable, even in different liquids. It can break strong chemical bonds and help build complex molecules, something rare metals usually do.

**Impact** – This aluminum compound is about 20,000 times cheaper than metals like platinum and palladium. Its special structure allows new types of chemical reactions that were not possible before. This could change how chemicals and materials are made, making processes cheaper, cleaner, and more sustainable.

**What's next step** – The research is still new, and the team will continue exploring how this aluminum compound can be used in industry. They hope it will lead to greener chemical production and new materials with useful properties.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have created a new aluminum compound that could replace costly rare metals, making chemical production cheaper and more environmentally friendly.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau composé d'aluminium pourrait remplacer les métaux rares et réduire les coûts
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques utilisent souvent des métaux rares et coûteux comme le platine pour fabriquer des produits chimiques et des matériaux. Ces métaux sont chers et difficiles à trouver car ils proviennent souvent de régions instables. Les chercheurs cherchent des alternatives moins chères et plus communes.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une équipe du King's College de Londres, dirigée par le Dr Clare Bakewell, a découvert une nouvelle forme d'aluminium avec une forme triangulaire unique composée de trois atomes d'aluminium. Ce nouveau composé est très réactif et stable, même dans différents liquides. Il peut casser des liaisons chimiques fortes et aider à construire des molécules complexes, ce que font habituellement les métaux rares.

**Impact** – Ce composé d'aluminium coûte environ 20 000 fois moins cher que des métaux comme le platine et le palladium. Sa structure spéciale permet de nouveaux types de réactions chimiques jusque-là impossibles. Cela pourrait changer la façon dont on fabrique les produits chimiques et matériaux, rendant les processus moins chers, plus propres et durables.

**Prochaine étape** – La recherche en est encore à ses débuts, et l'équipe va continuer à explorer comment ce composé d'aluminium peut être utilisé dans l'industrie. Ils espèrent qu'il permettra une production chimique plus écologique et de nouveaux matériaux utiles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont créé un nouveau composé d'aluminium qui pourrait remplacer les métaux rares coûteux, rendant la production chimique moins chère et plus respectueuse de l'environnement.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo compuesto de aluminio podría reemplazar metales raros y reducir costos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos suelen usar metales raros y caros como el platino para fabricar productos químicos y materiales. Estos metales son costosos y difíciles de encontrar porque provienen de regiones inestables. Los investigadores buscan alternativas más baratas y comunes.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo del King's College de Londres, liderado por la Dra. Clare Bakewell, descubrió una nueva forma de aluminio con una estructura triangular única formada por tres átomos de aluminio. Este nuevo compuesto es muy reactivo y estable, incluso en diferentes líquidos. Puede romper enlaces químicos fuertes y ayudar a construir moléculas complejas, algo que normalmente hacen los metales raros.

**Impacto** – Este compuesto de aluminio cuesta aproximadamente 20,000 veces menos que metales como el platino y el paladio. Su estructura especial permite nuevos tipos de reacciones químicas que antes no eran posibles. Esto podría cambiar la forma en que se fabrican productos químicos y materiales, haciendo los procesos más baratos, limpios y sostenibles.

**Próximo paso** – La investigación aún está en sus primeras etapas, y el equipo continuará explorando cómo se puede usar este compuesto de aluminio en la industria. Esperan que conduzca a una producción química más ecológica y a nuevos materiales útiles.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos han creado un nuevo compuesto de aluminio que podría reemplazar metales raros costosos, haciendo la producción química más barata y amigable con el medio ambiente.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of three interconnected glowing aluminum atoms forming a stable triangular ring, surrounded by ethene molecules gently weaving into the structure, symbolizing innovative chemical bonds and sustainable molecular growth against a soft, natural background of earth tones.

articles.title: The exotic particles that could finally break the Standard Model
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 01 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01387-x‘Penguin’ decays from CERN’s latest Large Hadron Collider experiment hint at weird new physics.
articles.content:
An engineer works on equipment for the LHCb experiment inside the tunnel of the Large Hadron Collider.Credit: Francis Demange/Gamma-Rapho via Getty

Physicists know that their elegant theoretical description of forces and particles – the Standard Model – must be incomplete, because there are a host of phenomena it cannot explain, such as the existence of dark matter.

But observations continue to confirm the model’s accuracy with ever greater precision. Even measurements that seemed to break the mould, such as a discrepancy in the mass of a particle called the W boson, have evaporated under further investigation.

Now, an analysis from an experiment at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) at CERN, Europe’s particle physics laboratory near Geneva, Switzerland, suggests that one deviation from the Standard Model has grown. It concerns the decay of particles called B mesons into other particles. The result, which has been accepted for publication in Physical Review Letters, is one of the last remaining anomalies for particle physicists, who look for new physics in the debris from proton-proton collisions that turn energy into matter.

Nature explores the latest findings from CERN’s LHCb experiment, and the exotic particles that could explain them.

What did the experiment find?

Rather than looking for new, heavy particles directly, LHCb looks indirectly for their subtle effects, including when they pop up fleetingly as “virtual particles” that influence decays. To look for these effects, researchers analyzed the frequency and angle at which particles emerge from decays, to check if they match with those predicted by the standard model. The new analysis looks at when a B meson — a particle composed of a bottom quark and another lighter quark — decays into another meson containing a strange quark, known as a kaon, as well as two muons (heavier cousins to the electron). They found that the angle at which the final products emerge from the decay disagree with those predicted by the Standard Model. Evidence for this anomaly has been growing since 2015.

How does this point to new physics?

Physicists think that this B meson decay — known as a penguin decay — should be particularly sensitive to as-yet undiscovered physics. (In 1977, British theorist John Ellis coined the term, owing to the resemblance of the decay’s diagram to a penguin, after losing a bet which forced him to include the word in his next paper1). The decay involves a quantum loop — in which a bottom quark changes into a strange quark, via a temporary transition into ‘virtual’ particles that pop in and out of existence. Quantum physics allows even heavy, non-Standard-Model particles, to fleetingly enter this loop and leave the final products with properties not possible from only known particles.

As this decay is so rare — around one in a billion B mesons decaying in this way — the impact of new particles should be easier to spot than in other, more common decays, where the signal would be drowned out.

Should we be excited?

The analysis includes around 650 billion decays amassed at the LHC during two runs between 2011 and 2018. Measurements of the angles of the particles emerging disagree with the standard model with a significance of around 4 sigma. This means that the chance that random noise from regular standard model processes would produce this signal is around 1 in 16,000, says William Barter, a particle physicist at the University of Edinburgh, UK, who works on LHCb. “This is among the most significant results of the last few years at the LHC,” says Barter. Particularly exciting is that the finding seems to be tentatively corroborated by another LHC experiment, CMS, which has observed a discrepancy in this B meson decay, albeit with lower statistical significance.

But excitement is tempered, he adds, because a rival decay involving particles called charm quarks can create the exact same products as the bottom-to-strange transition, and it is hard for theorists to predict precisely how these “charming penguins” would impact the angles of the final decay products. Theory suggests that this decay is unlikely to explain the full deviation from the standard model, but its existence gives room for caution.

If the signal is real, what new particles could explain it?

One possibility that could explain the discrepancy is if a particle known as Z’ (pronounced Z prime) is a virtual particle involved in breaking up the B mesons as part of the bottom-to-strange quark transition. Physicists hypothesize that this particle — which would be associated with a brand new, as-yet undiscovered force — would be similar to the Z boson, one of the two particles that mediates the weak nuclear force. But Z’ would heavier, and with a preference to interact with certain families of particles, says Ben Allanach, a theoretical physicist at the University of Cambridge, UK. The Z’ would mediate a force that discriminates between different “flavours” of particle, he adds. This theory could also help to explain why masses of particles in the standard model can be so radically different.

Another possibility is the existence of a leptoquark, a short-lived particle that, at high energies, is theorized to take on the properties of two families of particles — leptons and quarks. Leptoquarks provide another way in which bottom quarks could transition to strange quarks, and could also cause the decay angles observed, says Barter.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in particle physics with the potential to uncover new fundamental particles and forces beyond the Standard Model. This discovery has broad implications for our understanding of the universe and could lead to transformative advances in physics, benefiting society's knowledge and future technologies.
articles.personality_title: New particle decay hints at unknown physics beyond current theories
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists use the Standard Model to explain how tiny particles and forces work. But this model doesn’t explain everything, like dark matter. Researchers keep testing it to find where it might be wrong.

**What happened** – At CERN’s Large Hadron Collider (LHC) near Geneva, scientists studied how particles called B mesons break down, or decay. They looked closely at a rare process called a “penguin decay,” where a B meson turns into a kaon and two muons. The angles at which these particles appeared didn’t match predictions from the Standard Model. This difference has been growing clearer since 2015.

**Impact** – This result is important because it might show new particles or forces we don’t know yet. The penguin decay is very rare, so new effects are easier to spot. The chance that this finding is a random fluke is very low—about 1 in 16,000. Two different experiments at the LHC noticed similar strange results, making the discovery stronger.

**What's next step** – Scientists will keep collecting data and studying these decays to confirm if new particles like the Z’ boson or leptoquarks are causing the difference. They also need to understand other effects, like “charming penguins,” which might confuse the results. Future experiments will help clarify what is really happening.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A rare particle decay at CERN shows signs of unknown particles or forces, suggesting new physics beyond the Standard Model may be within reach.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle désintégration de particules suggère une physique inconnue au-delà des théories actuelles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques utilisent le Modèle Standard pour expliquer le fonctionnement des particules et des forces. Mais ce modèle n’explique pas tout, comme la matière noire. Les chercheurs le testent continuellement pour trouver ses limites.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Au Grand collisionneur de hadrons (LHC) du CERN près de Genève, des scientifiques ont étudié la désintégration des mésons B. Ils ont observé un processus rare appelé « désintégration pingouin », où un méson B se transforme en kaon et deux muons. Les angles d’apparition de ces particules ne correspondent pas aux prédictions du Modèle Standard. Cette différence s’est accentuée depuis 2015.

**Impact** – Ce résultat est important car il pourrait révéler des particules ou forces inconnues. La désintégration pingouin étant très rare, de nouveaux effets sont plus faciles à détecter. La probabilité que cette découverte soit un hasard est très faible — environ 1 sur 16 000. Deux expériences différentes au LHC ont observé des résultats similaires, renforçant la découverte.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à collecter des données et étudier ces désintégrations pour confirmer si de nouvelles particules comme le boson Z’ ou les leptoquarks sont responsables. Ils doivent aussi mieux comprendre d’autres effets, comme les « pingouins charmants », qui pourraient compliquer les résultats. Les futures expériences aideront à clarifier la situation.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Une désintégration rare au CERN montre des signes de particules ou forces inconnues, suggérant que la physique au-delà du Modèle Standard pourrait bientôt être découverte.
articles.personality_title_es: Una nueva desintegración de partículas apunta a una física desconocida más allá de las teorías actuales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos usan el Modelo Estándar para explicar cómo funcionan las partículas y fuerzas pequeñas. Pero este modelo no explica todo, como la materia oscura. Los investigadores lo prueban constantemente para encontrar sus límites.

**Qué pasó** – En el Gran Colisionador de Hadrones (LHC) del CERN cerca de Ginebra, los científicos estudiaron cómo se desintegran las partículas llamadas mesones B. Observaron un proceso raro llamado desintegración “pingüino”, donde un mesón B se convierte en un kaón y dos muones. Los ángulos en que aparecen estas partículas no coinciden con las predicciones del Modelo Estándar. Esta diferencia ha sido más clara desde 2015.

**Impacto** – Este resultado es importante porque podría mostrar partículas o fuerzas nuevas que no conocemos. La desintegración pingüino es muy rara, así que los nuevos efectos son más fáciles de detectar. La probabilidad de que este resultado sea un error es muy baja, alrededor de 1 en 16,000. Dos experimentos diferentes en el LHC vieron resultados parecidos, lo que hace la noticia más fuerte.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán recolectando datos y estudiando estas desintegraciones para confirmar si nuevas partículas como el bosón Z’ o los leptoquarks causan la diferencia. También necesitan entender otros efectos, como los “pingüinos encantadores”, que podrían complicar los resultados. Los próximos experimentos ayudarán a aclarar qué está pasando.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una desintegración rara en el CERN muestra señales de partículas o fuerzas desconocidas, sugiriendo que una nueva física más allá del Modelo Estándar podría estar cerca.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing, intricate quantum loop shaped like a stylized penguin made of flowing light strands, encircling abstract particle-like orbs representing B mesons, kaons, and muons, set against a softly illuminated tunnel resembling the Large Hadron Collider’s interior, with subtle hints of mysterious, shimmering particles symbolizing virtual exotic particles like Z’ bosons and leptoquarks gently influencing the loop’s path.

articles.title: An unorthodox version of quantum theory could reveal what reality is
articles.summary: The implications of quantum mechanics suggest reality isn't as solid as we think it is, but physicist David Bohm had a spin on the theory that restores reality. Columnist Karmela Padavic-Callaghan explores how we could test Bohmian mechanics – and if it will ever become more widely accepted
articles.content:
The implications of quantum mechanics suggest reality isn't as solid as we think it is, but physicist David Bohm had a spin on the theory that restores reality. Columnist Karmela Padavic-Callaghan explores how we could test Bohmian mechanics – and if it will ever become more widely accepted

A so-called pilot wave is key to David Bohm’s interpretation of quantum mechanics Courtesy of Daniel Harris and John Bush, MIT

Sometime in the 1940s, US physicist David Bohm decided that the only way to understand quantum mechanics was to write a book about it. He wanted it to offer the reader something they could understand in the “customary imaginative sense”, including an interpretation of the equations of quantum mechanics that didn’t require tangling with advanced mathematics. This quest stayed with him for over a decade, through years of personal and professional tumult, until he ended it by developing his own interpretation of quantum theory.

A pair of papers announced this “Bohmian mechanics” in 1952, but, save a few ardent supporters, it never really took off. Bohm had become a politically controversial figure, and his work was weighed down by some of that baggage, on top of its already heterodox approach to quantum physics. In 2025, however, an experiment with particles of light brought it back into focus – and reignited the idea that Bohm may have been onto something after all.

Bohm’s political trouble lay in his affiliation with several communist organisations during his doctoral studies and his unwillingness to testify and provide evidence against his colleagues in front of the House Un-American Activities Committee at the height of the post-second world war Red Scare. What made him somewhat heretical among physicists, on the other hand, was that he thought that reality is, well, real.

One of the big open questions in quantum mechanics is what exactly happens when an experimenter interacts with a quantum object. The mathematics on which the forefathers of quantum mechanics – such as Niels Bohr and Werner Heisenberg – built up the theory seem to accommodate an odd situation: while it is unobserved, an object is an existentially fuzzy mix of all of its possible states, but when it is observed, that cloud becomes one concrete state. Light and electrons give us the most extreme example of this. They exhibit “wave-particle duality”, where light that typically seems to be a wave can be corralled into materialising as a particle in some experiments, while other experiments can nudge objects that ought to be particles, such as electrons, to assume a wave-like nature instead.

How exactly this happens, and whether there is a physical mechanism that literally changes the object, or if the effect stems from how we process information, remains frustratingly unclear. But back up a little and we end up in an even murkier situation: what does quantum mechanics have to say about the reality of the world before an observation or a measurement can be made?

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

Heisenberg held that “the idea of an objective real world whose smallest parts exist objectively in the same sense as stones or trees exist, independently of whether or not we observe them… is impossible”. This view became quantum orthodoxy; now called the Copenhagen interpretation, it is widely held among physicists. But that wasn’t enough for Bohm. In the 1990s, he told New Scientist he found Bohr’s idea that quantum mechanics could offer only formulae that make great predictions, but doesn’t engage with a description of reality deeply unsatisfying. “I said, that’s not enough. I don’t think I would be very interested in science if that were all there was,” he said.

Bohm’s major intervention into quantum mechanics concerned a mathematical object called the wave function, which physicists use to represent an object’s quantum state. When a mathematical operation that represents a measurement is used on a wave function, it changes. This is infamously known as wave function collapse, and it was the first thing that alerted physicists to the idea that quantum objects may congeal from a cloud of probability into something more tangible. As Bohm saw it, the trouble was that the wave function itself wasn’t physical, but rather an abstract object that lived in a purely mathematical space, at least according to the likes of Bohr and Heisenberg. Bohm’s wave function, on the other hand, was never fuzzy, never collapsed and stood a chance of being a lot more real.

For instance, there is no wave-particle duality in Bohmian mechanics because particles are always particles and don’t have to be observed in some special way to show up to us in their particle form. But they still behave like waves in some experiments, so what did Bohm make of that? He proposed that it is because their behaviour is guided by a “pilot wave”, which is caused by fields similar to electromagnetic fields but associated with some set of as-yet undiscovered, inherently quantum forces.

Physicist David Bohm held the controversial view that reality is real Keystone/Getty Images

In Bohmian mechanics, the pilot wave is also to blame for everything odd that happens with quantum objects. If you can’t know something about a quantum object, for instance, it isn’t because that thing is fundamentally unknowable or because the world is probabilistic instead of deterministic, but because you disturbed the pilot wave. Details of the pilot wave are so-called hidden variables that we cannot access directly, but the pilot wave is physically there, a scaffolding for reality that exists even when absolutely no one is looking.

The price Bohm had to pay for going all in on pilot waves is that his idea by default allows for non-local effects – particles can stay inextricably connected across remarkable distances through “quantum entanglement”. Infamously, the idea of such non-locality was upsetting enough to Albert Einstein that he dismissed it entirely. There are many other so-called hidden variable theories that account for entanglement and other non-local effects, essentially trying to explain it away by invoking some unseen property or force. But Bohm was unbothered by the idea that incredibly distant parts of the cosmos could be sharing quantum states and affecting each other at all times.

His idea still relied on Erwin Schrödinger’s equation as the starting point of every quantum calculation, which is standard; he eliminated wave function collapse, which was bothersome to many; and some of his later philosophical ideas were even rather friendly to the notion that the whole cosmos may share some sort of implicit one-ness and connection.

Louis de Broglie, a pivotal figure in uncovering particles’ wave-like behaviour in the early days of quantum theory, formulated an idea similar to pilot waves in the 1920s, but was persuaded to abandon it by his peers. After Bohm independently came up with and further fleshed out this very similar framework, he was also met with resistance.

In 1989, philosopher Renée Weber, speaking to John Stewart Bell, a visionary physicist whose work on quantum entanglement also challenged our view of reality, asked why Bohmian mechanics had seemingly been so easy to ignore. Bell, who was a champion of Bohm’s work, demurred: “Let’s not go into that. That’s another question. That’s the psychology and history of physicists.” Bohm’s work always carried with it the shadow of his unfair political persecution. Yet there are also two major scientific problems with Bohmian mechanics.

The first gets to the core of how physics ought to work – can Bohmian mechanics suggest an experiment whose results would differentiate it from more orthodox interpretations of quantum mechanics? The word “interpretation” itself suggests that the answer should be no, because it is only distinct theories rather than interpretations of the same theory that can typically be told apart through experiments. But in July 2025, a study in the journal Nature suggested otherwise. Its title read “Energy-speed relationship of quantum particles challenges Bohmian mechanics”, offering a counterpoint to Bohm’s work more concrete than matters of philosophy or politics.

It didn’t start out that way, says Jan Klaers at the University of Twente in the Netherlands, who worked on the experiment. He and his colleagues set out to study the quantum phenomenon of tunnelling, where a particle manages to enter a space that ought to be forbidden to it, when they realised that they could actually infer something about Bohm’s work, too. They think they have identified a detail of his idea that could be experimentally tested after all.

Putting Bohm to the test

Their experiment was similar to a ball rolling down a hill and colliding with a wall. Outside of the quantum world, this is a straightforward situation, but in the quantum realm, the ball can “tunnel” into the wall. This process is distinct from the ball having enough energy to physically break a hole in the wall. With quantum tunnelling, there is simply some probability of the ball showing up inside the wall even if it isn’t very energetic at all. (In the mathematical world, the wave function of the ball “spills” into the wall.)

Klaers and his team made their own “ball” by shining a laser into a thin layer of a liquid filled with fluorescent dye molecules and sandwiched between two mirrors. The mirrors, the liquid and the molecules had to be there to make the massless photons behave as if they had mass, like a ball does, and the bottom mirror was also adorned with special nano-sized patterns that made the photons move along two pre-set parallel paths or “waveguides”. One was designed so that a photon would experience it as a downward ramp that ended in a bump – the wall at the bottom of a hill.

The researchers created photon after photon at the top of the hill and tracked what happened to them. Some jumped from one waveguide into another, and some tunnelled into the wall. Klaers says counting how many were hopping between the waveguides could serve as an internal clock for the system, with each hop like a tick – which let them determine the speed of the photons that were tunnelling through the barrier. The speeds they measured were rather high – thousands of kilometres per second – but when Klaers and his colleagues used Bohm’s approach to calculate what they ought to be, the answer was nearly zero. The discrepancy was large enough to put Bohmian mechanics into peril.

Not everyone agrees. Hui Wang at the University of Science and Technology of China says the velocity of tunnelling photons that could be calculated from the team’s setup cannot be directly compared to predictions of Bohm’s theory. Other quantities the researchers calculated from their measurements are, in his view, correct, and present no challenge to Bohmian mechanics, but their definition of speed is too close to its classical, rather than quantum, definition.

“I think Bohmian mechanics is still a promising interpretation of quantum mechanics,” says Wang. “Copenhagen interpretation is just a very powerful tool to correctly predict the experimental measurement results, but it provides very limited ‘physical reality’ of nature itself. Yet the core of science is to discover more of that reality in order to better understand nature. Bohmian mechanics is on this path.”

In fact, Wang says his own team is preparing a paper that will also offer a resolution to the second big problem with Bohmian mechanics, namely that it has historically not been applicable for objects that move close to the speed of light and therefore have to obey Einstein’s theory of special relativity – a pillar of physics that has withstood decades of testing. While their work has yet to be reviewed by other physicists, if successful, it could give Bohmian mechanics a real boost in the race to become the next best theory of physical reality. In that decades-old interview, Bell told Weber that he, essentially, felt similarly. Were it not for discrepancies with the mathematics of relativity, he would have simply adapted Bohm’s view as correct instead of seeing “a big, deep mystery in quantum mechanics”, he said.

While Klaers disagrees with Wang on the interpretation of his team’s data and how they used it to calculate photons’ speeds, he doesn’t think it’s fully lights out for all ideas like Bohm’s. There are two equations that are crucial for Bohmian mechanics, and they are the unavoidable: Schrödinger’s equation and the “guiding equation”, which is more specific to Bohm’s work. This second equation determines the velocity of any one particle from the configuration made by all particles. The experiment with tunnelling photons specifically points to issues with this guiding equation, says Klaers.

“There are many guiding equations possible, and you can come up with additional models that actually produce the same particle density and are in agreement with our speed measurements. So, it’s not really a distinction between Bohm mechanics and standard quantum mechanics. It’s really a question of, OK, this guiding equation that the standard Bohmian mechanics choose, is that actually the physically correct one?” he says. In a follow-up study, he and his colleagues have shown that tweaks to the guiding equation can push Bohm’s mathematics to match their experiment.

Is this actually good news for realists and Bohmians in the ranks of modern-day physicists? There is no straightforward answer. Finding a way to match a Bohmian theory to experimental evidence should add more credence to it, but a theory that must be repeatedly tweaked to catch up with experiments is at risk of not being sufficiently well-defined or fully complete. It is more likely that it is a stepping stone on the way to a theory that would have fewer ambiguities and more predictive power. In this way, Bohmian mechanics has been handed a new challenge – but at least it is staying in the ring.
articles.reasoning: The article discusses a significant scientific development in quantum mechanics, focusing on Bohmian mechanics, an alternative interpretation that could deepen our understanding of physical reality. It highlights recent experimental efforts that test and potentially validate aspects of this theory, which has broad implications for fundamental physics and our grasp of reality itself. The story is substantive, focused, and concerns a meaningful breakthrough with wide scientific and philosophical impact.
articles.personality_title: New experiments revive debate over reality in quantum physics
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum mechanics is a branch of physics that studies very tiny particles like light and electrons. It often shows strange behavior that makes it hard to say what is really happening. In the 1950s, physicist David Bohm suggested a different way to understand these particles, called Bohmian mechanics, which says particles are always real and guided by a 'pilot wave.' This idea was mostly ignored for many years.

**What happened** – In 2025, scientists performed a new experiment using light particles (photons) moving through tiny paths made with mirrors and special liquids. They measured how these photons tunneled through barriers, a strange quantum effect. The results showed speeds very different from what Bohm’s original theory predicted. This experiment challenged Bohmian mechanics but also opened the door to improving the theory.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows Bohmian mechanics can be tested with real experiments, not just ideas. It also means scientists might find a better way to explain how tiny particles behave and what reality is like at the smallest scales. Some researchers believe Bohm’s view could help us understand the universe better if its math is updated.

**What's next step** – Researchers are now working to fix problems in Bohmian mechanics, especially making it agree with Einstein’s theory of relativity, which explains how things move at very high speeds. More experiments and new versions of the theory may help it better match what we observe in the real world.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New experiments are testing and improving a long-ignored quantum theory that could change how we understand reality’s smallest parts.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles expériences relancent le débat sur la réalité en physique quantique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La mécanique quantique étudie les particules très petites comme la lumière et les électrons. Elle montre souvent des comportements étranges qui rendent difficile de comprendre ce qui se passe vraiment. Dans les années 1950, le physicien David Bohm a proposé une autre façon de comprendre ces particules, appelée mécanique bohmienne, qui dit que les particules sont toujours réelles et guidées par une « onde pilote ». Cette idée a été largement ignorée pendant longtemps.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2025, des scientifiques ont réalisé une nouvelle expérience avec des photons, des particules de lumière, qui se déplaçaient dans de minuscules chemins faits de miroirs et de liquides spéciaux. Ils ont mesuré comment ces photons traversaient des barrières grâce à un effet quantique appelé « effet tunnel ». Les résultats ont montré des vitesses très différentes de celles prédites par la théorie originale de Bohm. Cette expérience a remis en question la mécanique bohmienne mais a aussi ouvert la voie à son amélioration.

**Impact** – C’est important car cela montre que la mécanique bohmienne peut être testée par des expériences réelles, pas seulement par des idées. Cela signifie aussi que les scientifiques pourraient trouver une meilleure façon d’expliquer le comportement des particules minuscules et la nature de la réalité à cette échelle. Certains chercheurs pensent que la vision de Bohm pourrait aider à mieux comprendre l’univers si ses mathématiques sont mises à jour.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs travaillent maintenant à résoudre des problèmes dans la mécanique bohmienne, notamment pour la rendre compatible avec la théorie de la relativité d’Einstein, qui explique le mouvement à très grande vitesse. D’autres expériences et de nouvelles versions de la théorie pourraient l’aider à mieux correspondre aux observations.

**Résumé en une phrase** – De nouvelles expériences testent et améliorent une théorie quantique longtemps ignorée qui pourrait changer notre compréhension des plus petites parties de la réalité.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevos experimentos reavivan el debate sobre la realidad en la física cuántica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La mecánica cuántica es una rama de la física que estudia partículas muy pequeñas como la luz y los electrones. A menudo muestra comportamientos extraños que dificultan saber qué está pasando realmente. En los años 1950, el físico David Bohm propuso una forma diferente de entender estas partículas, llamada mecánica bohmiana, que dice que las partículas siempre son reales y están guiadas por una «onda piloto». Esta idea fue ignorada durante mucho tiempo.

**Qué pasó** – En 2025, científicos realizaron un nuevo experimento con partículas de luz (fotones) que se movían por caminos muy pequeños hechos con espejos y líquidos especiales. Midieron cómo estos fotones atravesaban barreras, un efecto cuántico extraño llamado tunelización. Los resultados mostraron velocidades muy diferentes a las que predijo la teoría original de Bohm. Este experimento desafió la mecánica bohmiana pero también abrió la puerta a mejorar la teoría.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que la mecánica bohmiana puede ser probada con experimentos reales, no solo con ideas. También significa que los científicos podrían encontrar una mejor manera de explicar cómo se comportan las partículas pequeñas y qué es la realidad a esta escala. Algunos investigadores creen que la visión de Bohm podría ayudar a entender mejor el universo si se actualizan sus matemáticas.

**Próximo paso** – Ahora los investigadores trabajan para corregir problemas en la mecánica bohmiana, especialmente para que sea compatible con la teoría de la relatividad de Einstein, que explica cómo se mueven las cosas a altas velocidades. Más experimentos y nuevas versiones de la teoría podrían ayudar a que coincida mejor con lo que observamos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Nuevos experimentos están probando y mejorando una teoría cuántica olvidada que podría cambiar cómo entendemos las partes más pequeñas de la realidad.
articles.image_prompt: A softly glowing, intricate network of gently undulating waves guiding a stream of glowing particles along two parallel, transparent pathways that curve gracefully through a calm, abstract cosmic landscape, symbolizing the pilot wave guiding quantum particles and the unseen forces shaping reality.
